common_name: Siratro (Purple Bush Bean, Phasey Bean, Purplebush)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun in any well-drained soil; Macroptilium atropurpureum establishes rapidly and fixes nitrogen from the air through root nodule bacteria. In zone 9, it often grows as a short-lived perennial — the tops may freeze back but roots and lower stems survive and resprout. Use as a living mulch between rows, a summer cover crop to mow and incorporate before fall planting, or an erosion-control cover on disturbed areas. Excellent nectar source for butterflies and small native bees. Somewhat invasive outside agricultural use in tropical areas — avoid planting near natural areas.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil — Siratro is one of the most drought-tolerant tropical legume cover crops available and establishes vigorously in hot, dry conditions where many other cover crops fail. Macroptilium atropurpureum is a twining, semi-erect to climbing legume native to Mexico, Central America, and South America in the Fabaceae family, widely used throughout the tropical and subtropical world as a cover crop, forage crop, and nitrogen-fixing groundcover. The plant produces trifoliate leaves (three broad leaflets, the middle one often lobed) on trailing to climbing stems that spread across the ground or climb on adjacent vegetation. Through the warm months, the plants produce clusters of small, vivid dark reddish-purple to maroon-red, butterfly-shaped (papilionaceous) flowers that are attractive to butterflies, native bees, and small pollinators. As a legume, Siratro fixes significant quantities of atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis with Bradyrhizobium bacteria, enriching the soil over the growing season. The dried, hay-form foliage is used as a high-protein livestock fodder in tropical agriculture. In zone 9 home gardens, Siratro is used as a summer living mulch between vegetable beds, as an annual cover to improve soil before fall crops, and as a wildflower-adjacent ornamental ground layer. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Macroptilium atropurpureum for Florida cover crops
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical cover crops for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens
USDA NRCS Plant Materials Center — Macroptilium atropurpureum: Siratro cover crop
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Legumes as Summer Cover Crops for Gulf Coast Louisiana

common_name: Perennial Soybean (Glycine, Soja Bean, Wight's Perennial Soybean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun in any well-drained soil with good air circulation; Neonotonia wightii is a vigorously climbing, twining vine that needs support or space to sprawl — control by mowing before seed set to prevent unwanted spread. It is a prolific nitrogen fixer when properly inoculated with appropriate Bradyrhizobium strains. In zone 9, the tops are killed by frost but root crowns often survive and resprout in spring. Use as a summer biomass crop mowed into garden beds before fall planting, or as a trellis cover crop to fix nitrogen while creating summer shade. Check local invasive status.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, moderately fertile soil with support for climbing — Perennial Soybean is a vigorous, twining vine that grows rapidly in warm conditions and fixes large quantities of atmospheric nitrogen, making it one of the most productive leguminous cover crops for zone 9-12. Neonotonia wightii (formerly Glycine wightii, Glycine javanica) is a perennial, twining legume vine native to sub-Saharan Africa in the Fabaceae family — the same tribe (Phaseoleae) and general lineage as the commercial soybean (Glycine max), hence the common name 'Perennial Soybean,' though not directly edible as a food crop. The plant produces trifoliate leaves on twining stems that climb vigorously over other vegetation, fences, and trellises, and produces small (0.25-inch), lavender-purple flowers in axillary racemes through the warm months — the flowers attracting bees and small pollinators. As a legume, it forms root nodules with nitrogen-fixing Bradyrhizobium bacteria, fixing significant quantities of atmospheric nitrogen. Neonotonia wightii has been extensively used in tropical and subtropical agriculture as a high-biomass forage crop (the foliage is highly nutritious for livestock) and as a cover crop and living mulch in commercial plantations (coffee, citrus, macadamia) throughout the tropics to fix nitrogen and suppress weeds. In zone 9 home gardens, it is best used as a summer biomass-building cover to improve soil before mowing and incorporating before fall vegetable planting. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Perennial Legumes as Cover Crops
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Neonotonia wightii for zone 9-10 cover crop use
USDA NRCS Plant Materials — Tropical Legume Cover Crops for the Gulf South
LSU AgCenter — Green Manure Legumes for Gulf Coast Louisiana gardens

common_name: Small-Flowered Cuphea (Candy Corn Cuphea, Micropetal Cuphea)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil after last frost; Cuphea micropetala is one of the more cold-tolerant Cuphea species, surviving temperatures to 25–28°F in established plants and often regrowning from the root crown after light freezes in zone 9. The orange-and-yellow tubular flowers are exceptionally attractive to hummingbirds and are produced almost continuously from spring through frost. The common name 'Candy Corn Cuphea' reflects the bicolored orange-yellow flower resembling candy corn. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Small-Flowered Cuphea blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates partial shade and is more drought-tolerant than many tropical-looking ornamental shrubs once established. Cuphea micropetala is a compact, semi-woody, perennial shrub native to Mexico and Guatemala in the Lythraceae family, one of the most cold-hardy and ornamentally valuable of the approximately 260 Cuphea species for zone 8-11 gardens. The plant produces a dense, upright to somewhat mounding form with narrow, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves and an exceptionally free-flowering habit — producing dozens to hundreds of tubular flowers at the leaf axils simultaneously through the warm months from spring through fall. Each flower is a small (0.5-inch), tubular, slightly curved structure in orange to orange-red with a yellow-green tip — a bicolored orange-and-yellow pattern that strongly resembles a piece of candy corn, explaining the marketing name 'Candy Corn Plant' sometimes applied to this species. The nectar-rich, tubular, hummingbird-adapted flowers attract Ruby-throated, Rufous, and other hummingbird species reliably, with hummingbirds visiting and revisiting a flowering Cuphea micropetala plant dozens of times daily during migration. Greater cold hardiness (to zone 8) than most ornamental Cuphea species makes this a particularly valuable selection for zone 9 gardens. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cuphea micropetala for Florida hummingbird gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Cuphea species for zone 8-9 Gulf Coast hummingbird plants
LSU AgCenter — Hummingbird-Attracting Plants for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Cuphea micropetala

common_name: Florida Beggarweed (Twisted Desmodium, Beggarweed, Tick Trefoil)

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, sandy to loamy soil; Florida Beggarweed grows naturally in sandy, sunny, disturbed ground throughout the Gulf Coast and Southeast and will often establish from wild seed in garden areas — it is frequently encountered as a beneficial volunteer rather than a planted crop. As a native annual legume, it fixes nitrogen, provides wildlife habitat (seeds eaten by quail and doves), and produces nectar for pollinators. The flat, segmented seed pods attach to clothing and animal fur (the 'beggar' quality) — plant away from high-traffic walkways.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, lean soil — Florida Beggarweed establishes most vigorously in open, sunny, sandy conditions and is a natural component of zone 8-12 Gulf Coast gardens that forms spontaneously in appropriate conditions. Desmodium tortuosum is a tall, annual to biennial legume native to Florida, the Gulf Coast states, the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central America in the Fabaceae family — one of the most ecologically valuable and practically useful native legumes for zone 8-12 gardens, historically grown as a 'forage crop' and soil-improving summer green manure throughout the southeastern US and recognized increasingly as a valuable native plant for wildlife and pollinator gardens. The plant produces upright, branching stems 3–6 feet tall with trifoliate leaves and, in summer and fall, clusters of small, typical legume flowers in pink to rose-purple that attract native bees, honeybees, and butterflies. As a legume, Florida Beggarweed fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis, improving sandy, depleted soils over the growing season. The flat, segmented seed pods ('loments') that mature in fall readily attach to animal fur and clothing — dispersing seeds wide and far, which explains the plant's wide natural distribution and common name. The seeds are an important wildlife food source — consumed heavily by bobwhite quail, doves, wild turkeys, and seed-eating songbirds throughout the fall and winter. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Desmodium tortuosum: Florida Beggarweed as cover crop
Florida Native Plant Society — Desmodium tortuosum species profile
USDA NRCS Plant Materials Center — Florida Beggarweed for wildlife and cover crop use
LSU AgCenter — Native Legumes as Summer Cover Crops for Gulf Coast Louisiana

common_name: Emarginate Dimerandra (Pink Rock Orchid, Dimerandra, Caribbean Rock Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on rough bark or cork, or in a small slatted basket with coarse bark medium in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun; Dimerandra emarginata is a relatively cold-tolerant epiphytic orchid from lowland tropical areas of the Caribbean and Central America, adapting to zone 9b-12 in warm, protected outdoor positions. It blooms throughout the warm months in zone 9-10 with cheerful small pink flowers. Water regularly in active growth; reduce in winter. The compact, reed-like growth habit makes it easy to mount and display. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light mounted on bark or in a small slatted basket — Emarginate Dimerandra thrives in the open-air conditions of a shaded outdoor porch in zone 9b-12 and benefits from excellent air circulation around its root zone. Dimerandra emarginata is a compact, reed-stemmed, sympodial epiphytic orchid native to the Caribbean (Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola, Puerto Rico), Mexico, and Central America in the Orchidaceae family, a member of the large and diverse Epidendreae tribe closely related to Epidendrum. The plant produces slender, cane-like pseudostems 10–20 inches tall from clustered, small pseudobulb bases, each cane clothed in overlapping, narrow, distichous (two-ranked) leaves. Through the warm months, terminal flowers emerge from the upper leaf axils — each flower small (0.75–1 inch), in a cheerful, clear rose-pink to cerise pink with a broad lip. While not a dramatic specimen, Dimerandra emarginata is a free-flowering, easy-care, cold-tolerant epiphytic orchid whose continuous summer bloom of fresh pink flowers on reed-like stems makes it an pleasant garden companion for zone 9-11 outdoor orchid collections. Its relative cold-tolerance (withstanding brief dips to 35–38°F on protected porches) makes it one of the epiphytic orchids most likely to succeed in zone 9 outdoor culture. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Dimerandra emarginata cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Dimerandra emarginata species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cold-tolerant small epiphytic orchids for zone 9-10
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Dimerandra (non-toxic to pets)
